topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights were too bright, a harsh, unforgiving glare that seemed to strip the color from everything. Elara stood near the entrance, her hands clenched so tightly her knuckles were white. Each brushstroke, each carefully layered shade of indigo and rust that was hers, now hung on pristine white walls, admired by a silent, judging crowd. A man in a tailored suit, whose smile didn't reach his eyes, gestured toward a canvas she had poured her soul into. "Magnificent," he murmured, his voice smooth as polished stone. Elara felt a sudden, cold pressure building behind her ribs, a physical tightening that made drawing a full breath a conscious effort. She shifted her weight, the expensive fabric of her dress suddenly feeling like a straitjacket. Every glance felt like an accusation, every appreciative nod a weight settling deeper into her chest. She wanted nothing more than to melt into the polished floor, to disappear into the shadows, but her feet remained rooted, unwilling to move from the periphery of her own creation.

[story 2]

I watched from across the room, the silence amplifying the frantic drumming in my ears. They were discussing the piece—my piece—with an easy familiarity that made my skin crawl. They spoke of composition and technique as if they were discussing the weather. My throat felt dry, a knot of panic constricting my ability to speak. I tried to walk toward the display, to snatch a small, folded sketch from a nearby pedestal, but a wave of nausea washed over me, and my legs felt suddenly heavy, unresponsive. A woman with dazzling jewelry leaned in close, her breath smelling faintly of mint, and whispered something that made the air thin out around me. I could only stare, my gaze locked on the surface of the painting, desperately searching for an escape route in the texture of the paint, a way to dissolve into the background noise of the room.

[story 3]

The velvet rope felt like a physical barrier, an invisible wall separating me from the art I had labored over. I paced the length of the gallery floor, the sound of my own hurried footsteps echoing unnervingly loud in the hushed space. When a critic approached, offering a polite, yet intensely scrutinizing look, I managed a tight, brittle smile. "It speaks volumes," he said, his tone suggesting he was dissecting something far more complex than pigment and canvas. I felt a tremor run through my hands, a desperate need to retreat, to find a corner where the scrutiny couldn't reach. My breath hitched, shallow and quick. I kept my eyes fixed on the distant exit sign, willing the moment to pass, wishing with a fierce intensity that the walls would simply close in around me, offering a final, dark reprieve.
